selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":19,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"carpenter","city_of_living":"Bologna","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"PhD","parent_2":"master's degree"},"current_marital_status":"single","children":0,"migration_status":"native-born Italian","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["gardening","football (calcio)","birdwatching","painting","baking"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":5,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":72,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":61,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":36,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":2,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"It’s honestly draining, this whole mess of fake content online. When you spend hours scrolling, like I do, you expect something real, something solid, like the grain in a piece of good oak or the predictable path of a bird migrating. But instead, you get this sludge. For me, dealing with it isn't about some grand technological fix; it’s about building mental walls, which takes a lot of conscious effort, something my high conscientiousness demands. I find myself having to be incredibly disciplined about what I let in. The trick, I think, is to treat social media less like a living room and more like a construction site—you inspect every beam before you let it bear weight. Before I share anything, or even before I fully believe something I read, I have to pause. I check the source; is it some flashy account with zero history, or is it something that has a verifiable track record, even if they’re just a small, local group I trust? It feels almost like quality control for my brain. Sometimes, when the noise gets too much, especially when I’m already feeling the weight of things, I just have to put the phone down entirely. That’s when I go out to the garden or just sit and sketch, something tangible. The constant stream of manufactured outrage or unbelievable stories just chips away at your focus. I’ve noticed that if I actively curate my feed—unfollowing the sensationalists and sticking to accounts that offer genuine skill, whether it’s someone showing a good woodworking joint or a reliable news source—the baseline level of anxiety drops. It’s a constant, deliberate pruning, like tending a garden to keep the weeds from choking the good plants. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: It’s honestly draining, this whole mess of fake content online. When you spend hours scrolling, like I do, you expect something real, something solid, like the grain in a piece of good oak or the predictable path of a bird migrating. But instead, you get this sludge. For me, dealing with it isn't about some grand technological fix; it’s about building mental walls, which takes a lot of conscious effort, something my high conscientiousness demands. I find myself having to be incredibly disciplined about what I let in. The trick, I think, is to treat social media less like a living room and more like a construction site—you inspect every beam before you let it bear weight. Before I share anything, or even before I fully believe something I read, I have to pause. I check the source; is it some flashy account with zero history, or is it something that has a verifiable track record, even if they’re just a small, local group I trust? It feels almost like quality control for my brain. Sometimes, when the noise gets too much, especially when I’m already feeling the weight of things, I just have to put the phone down entirely. That’s when I go out to the garden or just sit and sketch, something tangible. The constant stream of manufactured outrage or unbelievable stories just chips away at your focus. I’ve noticed that if I actively curate my feed—unfollowing the sensationalists and sticking to accounts that offer genuine skill, whether it’s someone showing a good woodworking joint or a reliable news source—the baseline level of anxiety drops. It’s a constant, deliberate pruning, like tending a garden to keep the weeds from choking the good plants. It requires that methodical approach, the kind you use when you’re measuring twice before you cut, otherwise, you just end up with shoddy work in your own head.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a conscientious, somewhat melancholic young man who values tangible quality (carpentry, gardening) over abstract noise. His low openness suggests a preference for established methods and caution. His depression suggests he is vulnerable to the negative effects of online negativity. Therefore, the advice must be highly pragmatic, disciplined, and focused on active filtering and self-care (analog activities) rather than abstract theory.
